fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, spinning up a ticket for the manifest generation work we discussed last sprint. Basically we need to build out the config-to-manifest pipeline for our k8s deployments. The core ask is that devs can pass in a structured config blob and get back deterministic summaries of what would be deployed — stuff like the workload spec, networking, scaling, storage, identity, monitoring hooks, and any custom extensions we bolt on.

The big pain point right now is that every team hand-rolls their own YAML and we keep seeing drift — missing labels, wrong selectors, incomplete port bindings, that kind of thing. We want one canonical way to do this.

A few things that need to work: required fields should blow up loudly when they're absent (similar to how we handled the null-value edge cases in the auth service config loader — you know the one), optional stuff should just silently fall back to defaults. Also the rollout strategy should only kick in when it's explicitly turned on, not default to something that could cause surprise restarts in prod.

We also need the CSI secret volume wiring and the managed cert stuff for GCP environments. The monitoring endpoint config should follow the same pattern we used for the service monitor setup in the platform team's last release.

Output needs to be line-oriented key=value so the deployment pipeline can parse it without a full YAML parser. Happy to clarify anything but let's get this scoped out.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: render(template: str, output: str, set: dict[str, str], values_files: list[str]) -> str

trigger_keywords: what happens when a required field is null
how are missing mandatory values handled
what error format is produced for absent required config
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a mandatory field such as containerImage.repository is explicitly set to null, the output must be exactly two lines: 'error=missing_required_value' followed by 'field=<field.name>', both newline-terminated. Optional fields like image pull policy may be omitted or null without triggering an error.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for the deployment container summary
how is the primary workload container rendered
what fields appear in deployment_core output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The deployment_core output reports: kind, name, replicas, container_name, image, command (empty string if none), args (empty string if none), host_aliases count, and then each container port as port_N_name, port_N_number, port_N_protocol — all as key=value lines.

trigger_keywords: how are environment variables injected
what does the env vars output look like
how do external env source references get rendered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The deployment_env_vars output starts with env_count=N, then lists each variable as env.<NAME>=<value>. For external source references (configmap or secret envFrom), the output uses ordered reference lines instead of direct name-value pairs.

trigger_keywords: how are configmap volumes named
what is the volume naming convention for config and secret mounts
how does a configmap get mounted as a volume
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a configMap or secret is mounted as a volume, the volume name is constructed as '<key>-volume' (e.g., 'dbsettings-volume'). Output reports volume_count, mount_count, each volume's type and source, and each mount's path and sub_path (null if not set).

trigger_keywords: how is a CSI secret volume rendered
what fields appear in the CSI volume output
how does the secret provider class get wired in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For CSI-backed secret provider volumes, the deployment_csi_secret output reports volume_count, then for each volume: type=csi, driver, read_only (true/false string), and secret_provider_class. The volume name follows the same '<key>-volume' convention.

trigger_keywords: how does shutdown delay affect lifecycle hooks
what happens when shutdownDelay is zero
when is lifecycle_present false
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A shutdownDelay of 0 produces lifecycle_present=false with empty post_start_command and pre_stop_command. A non-zero shutdownDelay generates a pre-stop sleep command. Explicit lifecycle hook commands override the delay-based generation.

trigger_keywords: how is scratch storage rendered
what does the deployment_volumes output contain
how are emptyDir volumes summarized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The deployment_volumes output reports mount_count, volume_count, termination_grace_period (null if unset), each mount as mount.<name>.path, and each volume as volume.<name>.type plus volume.<name>.detail. For memory-backed scratch storage the detail value is 'Memory'.

trigger_keywords: what happens when rollout strategy is disabled
how is deployment strategy rendered when turned off
what values appear for strategy fields when not enabled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When deploymentStrategy.enabled is false, all three strategy output fields are null: strategy_type=null, max_surge=null, max_unavailable=null. The strategy block is only populated when explicitly enabled.

trigger_keywords: how are deployment labels and annotations structured
what is the canary label behavior
what is the difference between pod_label_canary and deployment_label_canary
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The deployment_labels output includes deployment_label_canary (empty string for the workload-level label), pod_label_canary=main (for the pod template), extra_deployment_label and extra_pod_label from additionalDeploymentLabels/additionalPodLabels, and deployment_annotation plus pod_annotation from their respective annotation maps.

trigger_keywords: how is the service account rendered
what fields appear in service_account output
how is automount token reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The service_account output reports kind=ServiceAccount, name (from serviceAccount.name), automount_token (null if not set), annotation.foo (empty string if the foo annotation is absent), and image_pull_secret_count as an integer.

trigger_keywords: how is the internal service port rendered
what does service output look like
how are traffic policies reported for a ClusterIP service
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The service output reports kind=Service, name, type (e.g. ClusterIP), cluster_ip (null if not set), session_affinity (null if not set), external_traffic_policy (null if not set), internal_traffic_policy (null if not set), port_count, and each port as port_N_name, port_N_port, port_N_target.

trigger_keywords: how are ingress paths rendered
what does the ingress output format look like
how are numeric versus named backend ports distinguished
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The ingress output reports kind=Ingress, name, rule_count, tls_count, and for each rule: host, path_count, and per-path entries with path string, backend service name, port_number (integer or null), and port_name (string or null). Numeric ports populate port_number and set port_name=null; named ports do the reverse.

trigger_keywords: how is the managed certificate manifest rendered
what fields appear in managed_certificate output
how is the GCP cert resource summarized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The managed_certificate output reports kind=ManagedCertificate, name (from google.managedCertificate.name), and domains (comma-separated domain names from google.managedCertificate.domainName). The template key is 'gmc'.

trigger_keywords: how is the horizontal pod autoscaler rendered
what does hpa output look like
how are CPU and memory metrics listed in the HPA summary
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The hpa output reports kind=HorizontalPodAutoscaler, api_version=autoscaling/v2, min_replicas, max_replicas, metric_count, and for each metric: metric_N_resource (cpu or memory) and metric_N_average_utilization as an integer.

trigger_keywords: how is the pod disruption budget rendered
what does pdb output contain
when does the PDB manifest appear
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The pdb output reports kind=PodDisruptionBudget, name, and min_available. A PDB is only rendered when minPodsAvailable is greater than zero.

trigger_keywords: how is the service monitor rendered
what fields appear in service_monitor output
how are scrape endpoint details summarized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The service_monitor output reports kind=ServiceMonitor, name, endpoint_count, and for each endpoint: interval, timeout, path, port, and scheme. Configuration is typically supplied via a values file.

trigger_keywords: how are custom resources rendered
what does custom_resources output look like
how is user-supplied manifest data summarized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The custom_resources output reports manifest_count and for each manifest: manifest_N_kind and manifest_N_name. Custom resource data is supplied via a values file and rendered alongside generated resources.

trigger_keywords: how does the set parameter work
how do dot-notation keys get applied
how are nested config values overridden via the adapter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'set' map in the JSON adapter input accepts dot-notation keys (e.g. 'containerImage.repository') that are merged into the base configuration before rendering. The string value 'null' is interpreted as an explicit null/missing value, not as the literal string 'null'.

trigger_keywords: how do values_files get loaded
what is the precedence of values files versus set overrides
how does fixture file loading work in the adapter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The values_files list in the adapter input specifies YAML files to load before applying set overrides. Files are merged in order; later files take precedence over earlier ones, and set key-value pairs are applied last with the highest precedence.

trigger_keywords: what is the default release name
what name is used when no name is configured
where does release-linter come from
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default workload name used across all rendered manifests is 'release-linter'. This is the Helm release name injected at render time when no explicit name override is provided.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the null-value edge cases in the auth service config loader — you know the one
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1 required_and_optional_values: when containerImage.repository is set to the string 'null' in the set map it must be treated as a missing required value, producing error=missing_required_value and field=containerImage.repository output lines

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same pattern we used for the service monitor setup in the platform team's last release
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 16 service_monitor rendering: configuration is loaded from test/fixtures/service_monitor_values.yaml and the output is a line-oriented summary of kind, name, endpoint_count, and per-endpoint interval/timeout/path/port/scheme fields

context_pointers.vague_reference: the managed cert stuff for GCP environments
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 13 managed_certificate template (template key 'gmc'): renders a ManagedCertificate custom resource with fields google.managedCertificate.enabled, google.managedCertificate.name, and google.managedCertificate.domainName

context_pointers.vague_reference: the rollout strategy should only kick in when it's explicitly turned on
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 rollout_strategy: when deploymentStrategy.enabled=false all three output fields (strategy_type, max_surge, max_unavailable) must be null; no default rolling-update strategy is emitted

context_pointers.vague_reference: output needs to be line-oriented key=value so the deployment pipeline can parse it without a full YAML parser
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All render output modes (deployment_core, service, ingress, hpa, pdb, service_account, etc.) produce newline-terminated key=value plain-text lines with no YAML or JSON structure; null values are rendered as the literal string 'null'

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here — you'd need to check the existing implementation or the test fixtures under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and test/fixtures/ to confirm the expected behavior for that edge case.

feature10_service_account.json.description: Render a service account with the selected name, automount-token setting, annotations, and image-pull secrets.

feature11_service_networking.json.description: Render the internal service with its ports and optional traffic-policy, session-affinity, and cluster-IP settings.

cases.expected_output:
kind=Service
name=release-linter
type=ClusterIP
cluster_ip=null
session_affinity=null
external_traffic_policy=null
internal_traffic_policy=null
port_count=1
port_0_name=app
port_0_port=80
port_0_target=http

feature12_ingress_routing.json.description: Render ingress routing paths with numeric and named backend ports, including additional higher-priority or fallback paths.

cases.expected_output:
kind=Ingress
name=release-linter
rule_count=1
tls_count=0
rule_0_host=
rule_0_path_count=2
rule_0_path_0_path=/app
rule_0_path_0_service=release-linter
rule_0_path_0_port_number=80
rule_0_path_0_port_name=null
rule_0_path_1_path=/black-hole
rule_0_path_1_service=black-hole
rule_0_path_1_port_number=80
rule_0_path_1_port_name=null

feature13_managed_certificate.json.description: Render a managed TLS certificate manifest when certificate name and domain data are supplied.

feature14_horizontal_autoscaling.json.description: Render a horizontal autoscaler with replica bounds and CPU or memory utilization metrics.

cases.expected_output:
kind=HorizontalPodAutoscaler
api_version=autoscaling/v2
min_replicas=20
max_replicas=30
metric_count=2
metric_0_resource=cpu
metric_0_average_utilization=55
metric_1_resource=memory
metric_1_average_utilization=65

feature15_pod_disruption_budget.json.description: Render a pod disruption budget only when the minimum available pod count is greater than zero.

feature16_service_monitor.json.description: Render a service-monitoring manifest with endpoint scrape interval, timeout, path, port, and scheme data.

cases.expected_output:
kind=ServiceMonitor
name=release-linter
endpoint_count=1
endpoint_0_interval=10s
endpoint_0_timeout=10s
endpoint_0_path=/metrics
endpoint_0_port=http
endpoint_0_scheme=http

feature17_custom_resources.json.description: Render user-supplied custom resource manifests in the same output stream as the generated workload resources.

feature1_required_and_optional_values.js.description: Validate that mandatory workload identity and image fields are rejected when missing, while optional image policy data may be omitted.

feature2_deployment_container_spec.json.description: Render the primary workload pod container with the configured image, port, command, arguments, and host aliases.

cases.expected_output:
kind=Deployment
name=release-linter
replicas=1
container_name=linter
image=nginx:stable
command=
args=
host_aliases=0
port_0_name=http
port_0_number=80
port_0_protocol=TCP

feature3_environment_variables.json.description: Render direct environment variables and external environment source references into the workload container.

feature4_config_and_secret_volumes.json.description: Mount configuration maps and secret objects as pod volumes with optional item paths, file modes, and mount sub-paths.

feature5_csi_secret_volume.json.description: Render a CSI-backed secret-provider volume for workloads that retrieve secrets from an external provider.

feature6_lifecycle_hooks.json.description: Render container lifecycle hooks from shutdown-delay settings or explicit hook command configuration.

feature7_workload_storage.json.description: Render scratch storage, persistent storage, empty directory volumes, and termination grace-period settings into the workload pod.

cases.expected_output:
mount_count=1
volume_count=1
termination_grace_period=null
mount.scratch.path=/mnt/scratch
volume.scratch.type=emptyDir
volume.scratch.detail=Memory

feature8_rollout_strategy.json.description: Render the workload rollout strategy only when enabled, including rolling-update and recreate strategy variants.

feature9_metadata_labels_annotations.jso.description: Apply additional labels and annotations to workload and pod metadata while preserving the deployment-type label.

cases.expected_output:
deployment_label_canary=
pod_label_canary=main
extra_deployment_label=bar
extra_pod_label=baz
deployment_annotation=ann
pod_annotation=podann
